judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 is slightly stronger: it matches Report 1 on the prior-attempt/git-history and root-cause findings, but additionally explicitly documents that the serverless files were deleted by the revert (deliberate removal, not never set up), which Report 1 never states.

report_1.evidence: git show 1712565 (and its parents 454a21f, 597777b) documents that this exact migration (PLAT-31) was done and reverted after it caused a production outage.

report_2.evidence: git log --oneline --all -i --grep="revert" and git show 1712565 — confirms this exact migration (PLAT-31) was already attempted and reverted for a specific, documented reason.

report_1.evidence: The prior attempt (597777b) worked around it by opening a fresh unpooled connection per request, and the revert commit (1712565) states this "exhausted Postgres max_connections in production" and took the endpoint down under normal dashboard load.

report_2.evidence: the revert commit (1712565) states plainly that "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool" and that /reports/monthly "exhausted Postgres max_connections in production."

Recreate a serverless entry shim (backend/serverless_handler.py) ... (this file existed at commit 454a21f and was deleted by the revert). Recreate deploy/edgerunner.toml ... (also deleted by the revert; template is visible via git show 454a21f:deploy/edgerunner.toml).

report_1.evidence: Given this is a repeat of a known production incident, this should not be scoped as a routine infra swap — it should get sign-off from whoever owns production reliability for this service before a second attempt.

report_2.evidence: this decision — reattempting a previously-reverted approach — is not an engineer's unilateral call: whoever owns PLAT-31 (the ticket prefix suggests a "platform" team/owner) should explicitly sign off, informed that this exact path already failed once in production, before any Edgerunner work resumes.
